_meta.authoringNotes: Clinical case management per WHO 2009 dengue guidelines and 2023 WHO regional updates. Epidemiology per Bhatt et al. Nature 2013 (PMID 23563266) and WHO Dengue Fact Sheet 2024. Severe dengue pathophysiology per Halstead Lancet 2007 (PMID 17631701). Dengvaxia and Qdenga vaccine data per Sridhar et al. NEJM 2018 (PMID 29897851) and Biswal et al. NEJM 2020 (PMID 31693847).

h1Title: Dengue Fever: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Dengue fever is the world's most common mosquito-borne viral illness, with WHO modeling estimating 390 million infections each year, of which approximately 96 million are symptomatic. The virus is transmitted by Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes biting during the day in tropical and subtropical regions. Four virus serotypes (DENV-1 to DENV-4) co-circulate; infection with one provides lasting immunity to that serotype but not the others, and secondary infection with a different serotype substantially raises the risk of severe disease. Classic dengue presents 4-10 days after a bite with high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia (so-called break-bone fever), rash, and a dramatic platelet drop. Most patients recover within 7 days, but 0.5-1% progress to severe dengue with plasma leakage, hemorrhage, and shock — early recognition and fluid management cut mortality from over 20% to under 1%.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 390 million infections per year (96 million symptomatic) per Bhatt 2013 modeling; case reports rising globally year over year
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; severe dengue concentrated in children in long-endemic Asian countries and increasingly in adults in newly affected regions
keyStats.avgAge: Acute disease across all ages; peak severe dengue historically in children 4-14 in endemic Asia and Latin America
keyStats.globalCases: More than 5.2 million cases reported in 2019 (WHO), with continuing increases in 2023-2024; over 100 countries now have endemic transmission

definition: Dengue fever (ICD-10: A90 dengue fever without warning signs; A91 dengue hemorrhagic fever / severe dengue) is an arboviral infection caused by one of four dengue virus serotypes (DENV-1, DENV-2, DENV-3, DENV-4), all single-stranded RNA flaviviruses. The virus is transmitted by day-biting Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes; humans are the principal reservoir in the urban transmission cycle that drives most outbreaks. After a 4-10 day incubation period, infection causes a self-limited febrile illness in most patients, with high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, maculopapular rash, and a characteristic platelet drop. The 2009 WHO classification recognizes three categories: dengue without warning signs, dengue with warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hepatomegaly), and severe dengue (plasma leakage with shock or respiratory distress, severe bleeding, or severe organ impairment). The critical phase of dengue occurs as fever defervesces around day 3-7, when plasma leakage may begin abruptly. Infection with one serotype produces lifelong immunity against that serotype only; subsequent infection with a different serotype carries an approximately 7-fold higher risk of severe dengue (Halstead's antibody-dependent enhancement). Dengue is managed by primary care, infectious disease, emergency medicine, pediatric, and intensive care teams depending on severity. Two licensed vaccines (CYD-TDV/Dengvaxia and TAK-003/Qdenga) are available; both have specific eligibility criteria related to prior dengue exposure and age.

typesClassification.description: Most common form. Acute febrile illness with high fever, headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, and mild bleeding (petechiae, gum bleeding). Self-limited and managed at home with paracetamol and oral fluids.

Severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy or restlessness, hepatomegaly more than 2 cm, rapid platelet drop with rising hematocrit, or clinical fluid accumulation (ascites, pleural effusion). Requires hospitalization for intravenous fluid management and close monitoring.

Severe plasma leakage with shock or respiratory distress, severe bleeding, or severe organ impairment (liver, CNS, heart). Mortality 1-5% with good care, over 20% without. Requires intensive care and meticulous fluid management.

Original WHO classification grades I-IV based on hemorrhagic manifestations and shock. Grades III and IV (dengue shock syndrome) correspond to severe dengue. The 2009 classification is now preferred for clinical decision-making.

Atypical manifestations including encephalopathy, encephalitis, myocarditis, hepatitis, acute kidney injury, and acute respiratory distress syndrome. Recognized when standard categories do not capture the clinical presentation.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of high fever (often 39-40°C) lasting 2-7 days after a 4-10 day incubation following an Aedes mosquito bite.
Severe frontal or retro-orbital headache that worsens with eye movement.
Diffuse myalgia and arthralgia — the classic 'break-bone fever' — affecting back, limbs, and joints.
Maculopapular rash appearing day 3-5 of illness, often described as 'islands of white in a sea of red'.
Mild bleeding manifestations: petechiae, gum bleeding, epistaxis, mild menstrual irregularity.
Nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, and reduced oral intake.
Marked fatigue, anorexia, and altered taste that can persist for weeks after fever resolves.
Conjunctival injection and facial flushing, especially in the first 24-48 hours.
Tender hepatomegaly on examination in many patients during the febrile phase.
Defervescence around day 3-7 marking the start of the critical phase — clinicians watch closely for warning signs at this transition.
earlyWarningSigns: Severe abdominal pain or tenderness disproportionate to the febrile illness
Persistent vomiting (three or more times in 24 hours)
Mucosal bleeding (heavy gum bleeding, melena, hematuria) or new petechiae
Lethargy, restlessness, or altered mental status
Rapid platelet drop (under 100,000/µL with concurrent rising hematocrit) on serial laboratory monitoring
emergencySigns: Hypotension, weak rapid pulse, narrow pulse pressure (under 20 mmHg), cool clammy skin — dengue shock syndrome
Severe respiratory distress with hypoxia — pleural effusion, pulmonary edema, or ARDS
Massive hemorrhage (hematemesis, melena, hemoptysis, or per vaginal) — severe dengue with bleeding
Altered consciousness, seizures, or focal neurological deficits — dengue encephalopathy
Marked jaundice with rapidly worsening liver enzymes (AST/ALT above 1,000) — severe hepatitis variant of dengue

causes.cause: Bite of an infected Aedes aegypti or Aedes albopictus mosquito
causes.description: Day-biting Aedes mosquitoes acquire dengue virus by feeding on viremic humans and transmit to others 8-12 days later. A. aegypti is the principal urban vector; A. albopictus extends transmission into peri-urban and rural areas and parts of temperate climate zones.

causes.cause: Secondary infection with a different dengue serotype
causes.description: Lifelong immunity to one serotype does not protect against the other three. Subsequent infection with a different serotype carries approximately 7-fold higher risk of severe dengue through antibody-dependent enhancement, in which non-neutralizing cross-reactive antibodies facilitate virus entry into immune cells.

causes.cause: Outbreak conditions and high vector density
causes.description: Rainy season accumulation of water in containers, urban crowding, climate change extending Aedes range, and inadequate vector control drive periodic outbreaks. The 2023-2024 global surge has been notable in Bangladesh, India, Brazil, and southern Europe.

Dengue virus can cross the placenta and cause fetal loss, premature delivery, low birth weight, and severe neonatal infection if maternal viremia overlaps with delivery. Vertical transmission occurs in roughly 10-25% of pregnancies with peripartum viremia.

causes.cause: Blood transfusion, organ transplantation, needle-stick injury
causes.description: Rare nosocomial transmission routes documented. Blood services in endemic regions screen donors or defer those reporting recent fever and travel; healthcare workers must use universal precautions.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in an active transmission area
riskFactors.description: More than 100 countries across tropical and subtropical Asia, Latin America, Africa, the Pacific, and parts of Europe have endemic dengue transmission. WHO and CDC publish updated country-by-country risk maps.

riskFactors.factor: Prior dengue infection with a different serotype
riskFactors.description: Secondary infection with a different serotype carries approximately 7-fold higher risk of severe dengue, the strongest single risk factor for life-threatening disease.

riskFactors.factor: Age (children in endemic Asia, older adults in newer regions)
riskFactors.description: Severe dengue concentrated in children 4-14 years in long-endemic Asian countries (multiple serotypes co-circulate, secondary infection common). Older adults in newly affected regions carry higher mortality from severe dengue, in part because of comorbidities.
Pregnant women infected near term face elevated risk of premature delivery, low birth weight, and vertical transmission to the neonate. Antenatal screening is recommended in outbreak settings.

Diabetes, sickle cell disease, chronic kidney disease, and obesity are associated with more severe dengue and higher mortality. Asthma and atopy have been associated with dengue hemorrhagic fever in some studies.

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate vector control around the home
riskFactors.description: Standing water in flowerpots, tires, water-storage containers, blocked gutters, and discarded bottles support Aedes breeding. Eliminating these sources cuts household exposure and outbreak intensity dramatically.

riskFactors.factor: Outdoor activity during peak Aedes biting hours
riskFactors.description: Aedes mosquitoes bite primarily during daylight hours with peaks at dawn and dusk. Outdoor work, sports, and travel during these hours raise exposure substantially.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 390 million infections annually per Bhatt 2013 modeling; over 100 countries have endemic transmission.
Southeast Asia and the Western Pacific have historically borne the highest burden, accounting for over 70% of global cases.
Latin America: large outbreaks across Brazil, Mexico, Colombia, and Central America; Brazil reported over 1.6 million cases in 2024 alone.
South Asia: ongoing transmission in India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Nepal; Bangladesh experienced a record outbreak in 2023.
Europe and the US: travel-associated cases in the thousands; local outbreaks documented in France, Italy, Spain, Florida, and Texas in recent years.
diagnosisOverview: Suspect dengue in any patient with sudden high fever and at least two of: severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, mucosal bleeding, leukopenia, or thrombocytopenia, who has resided in or visited an active transmission area within the past two weeks. Concurrent testing for chikungunya, Zika, leptospirosis, malaria, and typhoid is essential in returning travelers because the differential is broad. Dengue NS1 antigen by ELISA or rapid lateral-flow test is highly sensitive in the first 5-7 days of illness; IgM antibody becomes positive 4-5 days after symptom onset and persists for 2-3 months; IgG is detectable from day 7 in primary infection (low titer) and earlier and at higher titer in secondary infection. RT-PCR is the most specific test but limited in availability outside reference laboratories. Daily complete blood count is the workhorse of clinical management — falling platelet count and rising hematocrit signal plasma leakage and the start of the critical phase. Liver enzymes, coagulation studies (PT, aPTT, fibrinogen), and serial vital signs (pulse rate, blood pressure, pulse pressure, capillary refill, urine output) drive fluid management. Imaging is reserved for evaluating effusion, ascites, or unusual presentations. Pediatric and obstetric cases warrant specialist input. The transition from febrile to critical phase around day 3-7 is the highest-risk period and warrants close monitoring or hospitalization in patients with warning signs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects dengue virus NS1 protein in the first 7 days of illness — the workhorse first-line test
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw or rapid finger-prick test. Rapid result in 15-30 minutes; ELISA returns same day or next day. Most sensitive in the first 5 days of fever.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects antibody response from day 4-5 onward; supports diagnosis when NS1 is negative
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results in 1-3 days. IgM positivity confirms recent infection; high IgG with low IgM suggests secondary infection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most specific test for dengue viral RNA and serotype identification; available in reference laboratories
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 3-7 days. Most sensitive in the first 7 days. Serotype identification is valuable for epidemiology and vaccine decision-making.

diagnosticTests.test: Serial complete blood count (platelets and hematocrit)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Tracks platelet decline and hemoconcentration — the core indicators of plasma leakage and the critical phase
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draws every 12-24 hours during the febrile phase and more frequently in the critical phase. Falling platelet count and rising hematocrit signal escalation in care needs.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and coagulation studies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents hepatic involvement and coagulopathy, common in moderate-severe dengue
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. AST and ALT often rise to 100-500 U/L; AST/ALT above 1,000 suggests severe hepatic dengue. Coagulopathy may require fresh-frozen plasma in bleeding patients.

diagnosticTests.test: Bedside ultrasound for effusion and ascites
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects plasma leakage manifesting as pleural effusion, ascites, or gallbladder wall thickening
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed at the bedside in 10-15 minutes. Pleural effusion right side and gallbladder wall thickening over 5 mm are recognized indicators of plasma leakage.

diagnosticTests.test: Concurrent testing for chikungunya, Zika, malaria, typhoid, and leptospirosis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes dengue from major co-circulating differential diagnoses with different management implications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed from the same blood sample used for dengue testing. Critical in returning travelers and during regional outbreaks.

treatmentOverview: No specific antiviral therapy exists for dengue. Management is meticulous supportive care based on the 2009 WHO classification and serial clinical-laboratory monitoring. Outpatients with dengue without warning signs receive paracetamol for fever (avoid aspirin and NSAIDs, which can worsen bleeding), oral fluids targeted to 1-2 mL/kg/hour urine output, rest, and bite avoidance to prevent onward transmission. Daily review and CBC continue until at least 48 hours after fever resolves. Patients with warning signs require hospitalization for crystalloid fluid management — typically Ringer's lactate or 0.9% saline starting at 5-7 mL/kg/hour, titrated to hourly hematocrit, blood pressure, and urine output. Severe dengue requires intensive care with aggressive fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, followed by 10 mL/kg colloid if shock persists), correction of metabolic acidosis, transfusion of packed red cells for hematocrit drop with bleeding, and platelet transfusion only for significant bleeding (prophylactic platelet transfusion is not recommended for thrombocytopenia alone). Fluid overload is the most common iatrogenic complication and is managed by titrating downward as hematocrit falls and urine output recovers; the critical phase lasts 24-48 hours. There is no role for corticosteroids, intravenous immunoglobulin, or antiviral drugs in routine management. The CYD-TDV (Dengvaxia) vaccine is recommended only for individuals aged 9-45 with documented prior dengue infection because of increased severe disease risk in seronegative recipients; TAK-003 (Qdenga) is licensed in multiple countries for ages 4-60 and does not require prior dengue infection (though pre-vaccination screening is still recommended in some settings).

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 500-1000 mg every 4-6 hours (max 4 g/day adults; weight-based in children)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesic and antipyretic. Aspirin and NSAIDs are contraindicated because they increase bleeding risk in dengue.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces fever and pain in over 80% of patients while avoiding bleeding risk

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution for dengue without warning signs
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted to maintain urine output of 1-2 mL/kg/hour. Encourages 2-3 liters per day in adults and weight-based volumes in children. Replaces electrolytes lost from fever and gastrointestinal symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate in over 95% of patients without warning signs

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous crystalloid fluids (Ringer's lactate, 0.9% saline)
medicalTreatments.description: Cornerstone of inpatient management. Starting rate typically 5-7 mL/kg/hour for dengue with warning signs, titrated downward as the critical phase resolves. Aggressive boluses (20 mL/kg) for shock.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severe dengue mortality from over 20% to under 1% when titrated to clinical parameters

medicalTreatments.name: Colloid solutions (gelatin, starch alternatives) for refractory shock
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe dengue with persistent shock despite crystalloid resuscitation. Used as 10 mL/kg boluses with close monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores hemodynamics in approximately 60-80% of patients with persistent shock after crystalloid

medicalTreatments.name: Packed red blood cell transfusion for hematocrit drop with bleeding
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated when hematocrit falls below baseline and the patient has significant clinical bleeding (hematemesis, melena, hemoptysis) or persistent shock despite fluid resuscitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores oxygen-carrying capacity and supports recovery from severe bleeding

medicalTreatments.name: Dengue vaccine (Qdenga TAK-003 or Dengvaxia CYD-TDV)
medicalTreatments.description: TAK-003 (Qdenga) licensed in multiple countries for ages 4-60; CYD-TDV (Dengvaxia) restricted to dengue-seropositive individuals aged 9-45. Decisions guided by national policy and pre-vaccination serostatus where required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Qdenga: approximately 80% efficacy against virologically confirmed dengue in seropositive recipients; 73% in seronegative across 18 months; Dengvaxia: protective in seropositive recipients with increased severe disease risk in seronegatives

surgicalOptions.name: Therapeutic thoracentesis or paracentesis for symptomatic effusion or ascites
surgicalOptions.description: Rarely needed in severe dengue with large symptomatic pleural effusions or tense ascites. Performed when respiratory or hemodynamic compromise persists despite optimal fluid management.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in over 90% of treated effusions; underlying plasma leakage typically resolves spontaneously within 24-48 hours

alternativeTreatments.name: Carica papaya leaf extract (traditional remedy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Widely used in South and Southeast Asia for thrombocytopenia. Some small trials suggest modest increases in platelet count but high-quality evidence is limited and it does not replace standard supportive care.

alternativeTreatments.name: Structured rest and gradual reintroduction of activity during convalescence
alternativeTreatments.description: Fatigue, weakness, and reduced exercise tolerance commonly persist for weeks after acute dengue. Graded return to work and exercise reduces post-dengue fatigue syndrome impact.

whySeeSpecialist: Infectious disease and pediatric specialist input is recommended for any patient with warning signs, severe dengue, pregnancy, or significant comorbidities. Critical care involvement is essential for dengue shock syndrome. Travel medicine clinics support vaccine decisions in eligible patients.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for clinicians and hospitals familiar with WHO 2009 dengue case management, with established fluid management protocols and rapid access to NS1 and serology testing. In endemic regions, designated dengue care wards offer the best outcomes. Ensure pediatric and obstetric services are available for relevant patients.
hospitalCriteria: Access to dengue NS1 antigen, IgM serology, and ideally RT-PCR
24-hour laboratory for hematology and biochemistry monitoring
Pediatric and intensive care services for severe cases
Defined dengue case management protocols based on WHO 2009 framework
Blood transfusion services and platelet availability
keyFacilities: Adult and pediatric intensive care units
Travel medicine and pre-travel vaccination clinics
Public health surveillance and vector control programs
preventionStrategies: Use insect repellent containing DEET (20-30%), picaridin, IR3535, or oil of lemon eucalyptus on exposed skin during the day in endemic regions and reapply per label.
Wear long sleeves, long trousers, and permethrin-treated clothing during peak Aedes activity (dawn and dusk).
Stay in air-conditioned or screened accommodation; sleep under a permethrin-treated bed net if the room is not screened.
Eliminate breeding sites around the home: empty flowerpots, change water in bird baths twice weekly, cover or discard tires and containers, clear blocked gutters.
Consider the dengue vaccine if you meet eligibility criteria (Qdenga for ages 4-60 in licensed countries; Dengvaxia for ages 9-45 with documented prior dengue infection).
Use mosquito protection during the first week of illness if infected to prevent onward transmission to vectors and family members.
lifestyleModifications: Rest during the acute and critical phases; gradually resume normal activity over 1-2 weeks of convalescence.
Drink at least 2-3 liters of fluid daily during the febrile phase and as advised by the clinician.
Inspect the skin daily for new petechiae or bleeding; report any to the clinician promptly.
Track symptoms and seek urgent care for warning signs (severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy).
Continue insect repellent and screened housing for at least one week after recovery to prevent re-infection or onward transmission.
Disclose recent travel and dengue history at any future medical encounter, especially before vaccination decisions or transfusion.

recommended: Adequate oral fluids (2-3 liters per day in adults) — water, oral rehydration solution, fresh fruit juices
Easily digestible foods (rice, soft fruits, broth, plain yogurt) during the febrile phase
Iron-rich foods during convalescence if anemia is documented
Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support recovery
avoid: Aspirin and NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac, mefenamic acid) — increase bleeding risk in dengue
Alcohol — interacts with hepatic dysfunction common in dengue
Spicy, oily, or heavy foods during the acute phase that may worsen nausea
Untested 'miracle remedies' or untreated water that may add an enteric infection

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the acute and critical phases. Light walking once fever has resolved and warning signs have settled. Avoid strenuous exercise for at least 1-2 weeks during convalescence to allow full hematologic recovery. Athletes returning from dengue should have a normal CBC and clinician clearance before returning to high-intensity training.
dailyManagement: Take paracetamol for fever and pain; never use aspirin or NSAIDs unless cleared by your doctor.
Drink at least 2-3 liters of fluid each day, more if losses are high.
Monitor for warning signs: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, sudden cooling, dizziness on standing.
Attend daily review and laboratory testing as advised during the febrile and critical phases.
Use mosquito repellent and stay in screened accommodation through the first week to prevent onward transmission.
Resume normal activity gradually over 1-2 weeks of convalescence.
prognosis: Acute dengue without warning signs has an excellent prognosis: more than 99% of patients recover within 1-2 weeks. Mortality in severe dengue falls from over 20% without specific care to under 1% with adherence to WHO fluid management protocols. The critical phase around days 3-7 is the highest-risk period; once a patient has had 48 hours of stable hemodynamics and rising platelets, recovery is reliable. Long-term sequelae are uncommon; some patients report post-dengue fatigue syndrome with weakness, hair loss, and reduced exercise tolerance for weeks to a few months. Infection produces lifelong immunity to that serotype only; secondary infection with a different serotype carries the highest risk for severe disease. The widening adoption of TAK-003 (Qdenga) vaccine has the potential to change population-level outcomes over the next decade.
recoveryTimeline: Acute fever resolves over 2-7 days. Critical phase lasts 24-48 hours after defervescence. Platelet count typically recovers over 5-10 days. Hematocrit normalizes over 2-3 days after the critical phase. Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance may persist for 2-6 weeks during convalescence.
complications: Severe dengue with shock and respiratory distress — mortality 1-5% with optimal care, over 20% without.
Severe hemorrhage — gastrointestinal, intracranial, or pulmonary bleeding requiring blood product support.
Severe organ dysfunction — myocarditis, encephalitis, severe hepatitis, acute kidney injury.
Fluid overload from over-aggressive fluid resuscitation, causing pulmonary edema and prolonged hospitalization.
Maternal and fetal complications including premature delivery, low birth weight, and rare vertical transmission.
Post-dengue fatigue syndrome lasting weeks to a few months in some patients.

supportResources.name: WHO Dengue and Severe Dengue Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, case management framework, vaccine guidance, and emergency response information.

supportResources.name: CDC Dengue
supportResources.description: US public health guidance on diagnosis, prevention, traveler advisories, vaccine recommendations, and laboratory testing.

supportResources.name: Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) Dengue
supportResources.description: Regional public health information for the Americas, where major dengue outbreaks are increasingly common.

supportResources.name: Dengue Tracker (ProMED-mail) and WHO Disease Outbreak News
supportResources.description: Real-time global outbreak reporting and surveillance system tracking dengue and other infectious disease outbreaks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chikungunya
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chikungunya causes more severe and prolonged joint pain than dengue, with less prominent thrombocytopenia and no plasma leakage. Both are transmitted by the same Aedes mosquito and frequently co-circulate; concurrent testing is mandatory.

confusedWithConditions.name: Zika Virus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Zika usually has milder fever, prominent conjunctivitis, and a rash similar to dengue but without significant thrombocytopenia or plasma leakage. The major Zika risks are congenital infection and Guillain-Barre syndrome. All three Aedes-borne diseases should be tested together when symptoms overlap.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted by night-biting Anopheles mosquitoes and produces cyclical fever rather than a single 5-7 day febrile illness. Thick and thin blood films or rapid antigen tests confirm malaria, which requires antimalarial therapy rather than supportive care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis is a bacterial zoonosis transmitted through contaminated water, presenting with fever, myalgia (especially calves), conjunctival suffusion, jaundice, and renal failure (Weil's disease). It responds to doxycycline or penicillin and is often co-tested with dengue in tropical fever workups.

relatedConditions.name: Chikungunya
relatedConditions.relevance: Same Aedes mosquito vector and overlapping clinical features; concurrent testing is mandatory in endemic regions.

relatedConditions.name: Zika Virus
relatedConditions.relevance: Same vector and overlapping clinical features; differential testing is required in any compatible patient.

relatedConditions.name: Yellow Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Another flavivirus transmitted by mosquitoes and a key differential for any febrile illness with hepatic involvement in returning travelers.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Another mosquito-borne febrile illness requiring distinct treatment; often considered together in tropical fever workups.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common dengue mimic in tropical regions; specific antibiotic therapy is curative and prompt distinction is important.

faqs.question: What is dengue fever?
faqs.answer: Dengue fever is a viral disease caused by one of four dengue virus serotypes and transmitted by day-biting Aedes mosquitoes. It causes sudden high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia, rash, and a characteristic platelet drop. Most patients recover within a week but 0.5-1% develop severe dengue.

faqs.question: How is dengue transmitted?
faqs.answer: Dengue is transmitted through the bite of infected Aedes aegypti or Aedes albopictus mosquitoes that bite during the day. Vertical mother-to-fetus transmission can occur near delivery. Rare nosocomial routes include blood transfusion, organ transplantation, and needle-stick injury. Person-to-person spread does not occur directly.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of severe dengue?
faqs.answer: Severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding (gums, nose, gut), lethargy or restlessness, hepatomegaly more than 2 cm, rapid platelet drop with rising hematocrit, and clinical fluid accumulation (ascites or pleural effusion) all warrant hospitalization. They mark the start of the critical phase around day 3-7.

faqs.question: Can dengue kill you?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe dengue with shock, respiratory distress, severe bleeding, or organ failure can be fatal. Mortality is under 1% with optimal supportive care but exceeds 20% without it. Early recognition and meticulous fluid management are the most important determinants of survival.

faqs.question: How is dengue diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Dengue NS1 antigen test is the first-line test in the first 7 days; IgM antibodies become positive from day 4-5. RT-PCR is the most specific test where available. Daily complete blood count tracks platelet count and hematocrit. Concurrent testing for chikungunya, Zika, and malaria is essential.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for dengue?
faqs.answer: Treatment is supportive: paracetamol for fever, oral fluids, rest, and serial monitoring. Aspirin and NSAIDs are contraindicated because they worsen bleeding. Hospitalization with intravenous fluid management is needed for warning signs or severe dengue. There is no specific antiviral therapy.

faqs.question: Can you get dengue more than once?
faqs.answer: Yes. Each of the four dengue serotypes (DENV-1 to DENV-4) produces lifelong immunity to itself only. A second infection with a different serotype carries approximately 7-fold higher risk of severe dengue through antibody-dependent enhancement. People in endemic areas may have multiple infections in a lifetime.

faqs.question: Is there a dengue vaccine?
faqs.answer: Yes. TAK-003 (Qdenga) is licensed in many countries for ages 4-60 and offers approximately 80% protection against confirmed dengue in seropositive recipients. CYD-TDV (Dengvaxia) is licensed for ages 9-45 with documented prior dengue infection. Eligibility and recommendations vary by country.

faqs.question: Should pregnant women get dengue vaccine?
faqs.answer: Both CYD-TDV (Dengvaxia) and TAK-003 (Qdenga) are live attenuated vaccines and are contraindicated in pregnancy. Pregnant women should rely on strict mosquito-bite prevention. Prior to becoming pregnant, eligible women can discuss vaccination with their travel medicine provider.

faqs.question: Why are aspirin and ibuprofen avoided in dengue?
faqs.answer: Aspirin and NSAIDs inhibit platelet function and impair gastric mucosal protection, both of which raise bleeding risk in dengue. Dengue already lowers platelet counts and damages capillary integrity, so concomitant NSAID use can precipitate serious gastrointestinal or other hemorrhage. Paracetamol is the safe alternative.

faqs.question: How long does dengue fever last?
faqs.answer: Acute fever lasts 2-7 days. The critical phase begins as fever resolves and lasts 24-48 hours. Full recovery from acute illness takes 1-2 weeks, with fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance persisting for 2-6 weeks in some patients. Severe disease may extend hospitalization to 5-10 days.

faqs.question: Can children get dengue?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children are highly susceptible and historically have shown the highest rates of severe dengue in long-endemic Asian countries. Severe dengue in children may present subtly with lethargy and reduced oral intake rather than obvious bleeding; close clinical observation is essential.

faqs.question: How do you prevent dengue?
faqs.answer: Use DEET, picaridin, or IR3535 insect repellent during the day, wear long sleeves and trousers, stay in air-conditioned or screened accommodation, and eliminate standing water around the home. Vaccination is available for eligible individuals in many countries. Vector control by public health authorities is essential during outbreaks.

faqs.question: Does papaya leaf extract help dengue?
faqs.answer: Some small clinical trials suggest modest improvement in platelet recovery with Carica papaya leaf extract, but high-quality evidence is limited and it should not replace standard supportive care and fluid management. Patients using papaya leaf preparations should still attend medical review and laboratory monitoring.

faqs.question: How is dengue different from flu?
faqs.answer: Both cause fever and myalgia, but dengue is mosquito-borne and produces retro-orbital pain, severe joint pain (break-bone fever), maculopapular rash, and a characteristic platelet drop. Influenza is droplet-spread, has prominent respiratory symptoms (cough, sore throat, nasal congestion), and responds to oseltamivir within 48 hours of onset.

faqs.question: Can you exercise after dengue?
faqs.answer: Avoid strenuous exercise during the acute and critical phases. Light walking is appropriate once fever has resolved and warning signs have settled. Full return to high-intensity exercise should wait at least 1-2 weeks during convalescence, with normalization of CBC and clinician clearance for athletes.

faqs.question: Is dengue contagious from person to person?
faqs.answer: Dengue is not contagious through normal contact, droplets, or sexual transmission. It requires the Aedes mosquito vector to complete the transmission cycle. Vertical transmission from mother to fetus near delivery and rare nosocomial transmission via transfusion or needle-stick are exceptions.

faqs.question: What is dengue hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: Dengue hemorrhagic fever is the legacy 1997 WHO classification for severe dengue with bleeding, hemoconcentration, and plasma leakage. The 2009 WHO classification renamed grades III and IV as 'severe dengue' to better guide bedside care. Both terms describe the most dangerous forms of dengue.

faqs.question: Why are platelets low in dengue?
faqs.answer: Dengue virus infects megakaryocytes and platelets, induces antibody-mediated platelet destruction, and triggers bone-marrow suppression. The platelet count typically falls dramatically over the febrile and critical phases, sometimes below 20,000/µL, before recovering as the infection resolves. Most patients do not need platelet transfusion.

faqs.question: When should I go to hospital for dengue?
faqs.answer: Go to hospital immediately for severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, dizziness on standing, cold extremities, or any of the WHO warning signs. Patients with comorbidities, pregnancy, age over 65, or fast deterioration should also be assessed in hospital regardless of warning signs.

faqs.question: Can dengue cause long-term problems?
faqs.answer: Most patients recover fully. Some experience post-dengue fatigue syndrome with weakness, hair loss, and reduced exercise tolerance for weeks to months. Severe dengue with prolonged shock or organ failure can have residual effects on kidney, heart, or brain function in a small minority of survivors.

metaTitle: Dengue Fever: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Dengue fever: mosquito-borne illness with high fever, severe joint pain, and bleeding risk. Symptoms, warning signs, treatment, vaccines and prevention.
